context.description: A nonfiction book page argues that neuroscience is more useful than psychology for understanding free will. It introduces Benjamin Libet's early decision-making experiments and John-Dylan Haynes's later fMRI findings predicting choices several seconds before conscious awareness. The page frames these results as a major turning point that renewed public and philosophical debate about free will.
organizations: Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences
normalization_notes: Classified as a nonfiction chapter page on neuroscience and free will.

rows.paragraph_text: The author contrasts psychology with neuroscience and argues that neuroscience offers the more relevant path for the journey ahead.
Benjamin Libet's 1970s experiments suggested measurable neural activity precedes conscious decisions by up to half a second, a claim that remained controversial.
John-Dylan Haynes later used fMRI at the Max Planck Institute in Leipzig to predict choices up to seven seconds before conscious decision with about 60% accuracy, reviving debate about free will.
The findings are presented as having energized research and broadened philosophical discussion beyond science.

content.free_text: book_page consciousness_and_mind neuroscience_reflection Neuroscience and free will; Libet and Haynes experiments; renewed debate on conscious decision-making.
